name: VTE - Anticoagulant WITHOUT Diagnosis (Negation / AFib Cohort)
description: **Negation test.** Find patients on therapeutic anticoagulants who do NOT have a venous thromboembolism diagnosis. These are typically patients on long-term anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation, mechanical valves, or post-op DVT prophylaxis — clinically distinct from VTE patients but carrying the same bleeding risk. FHIR R4 doesn't natively support `NOT EXISTS` in queries, so the canonical solution is a two-query subtraction: anticoag patients minus VTE-diagnosed patients. Tests whether LLM understands the FHIR negation idiom.
prompt: Find patients prescribed anticoagulant medications (warfarin, apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, or enoxaparin) who do NOT have a formal venous thromboembolism diagnosis. The intent is to identify patients on long-term anticoagulation for non-VTE indications such as atrial fibrillation stroke prevention or mechanical heart valve prophylaxis.

prompts.naive: Find patients on blood thinners without a clot diagnosis.
prompts.broad: Find patients prescribed therapeutic anticoagulants (warfarin, DOACs, LMWH) who do NOT have a venous thromboembolism diagnosis. FHIR R4 does not natively support negative chains, so this requires a two-query subtraction approach: patients on anticoagulants MINUS patients with VTE diagnoses.
prompts.expert: I need set difference: MedicationRequest?code=anticoags MINUS Condition?code=VTE-codes (at the patient level). FHIR doesn't support `NOT EXISTS` natively, so two queries are required: (1) `MedicationRequest?code=http://www.nlm.nih.gov/research/umls/rxnorm|11289,1364430,1114195,1037042,67108` to get anticoag patients, (2) `Condition?code=http://snomed.info/sct|128053003,234044007,281595001,59282003,234049002` to get VTE-diagnosed patients, then return query 1 patient set MINUS query 2 patient set.

metadata.algorithm_path_description: Set difference (medication patients) MINUS (diagnosed patients). FHIR R4 has no native NOT EXISTS — requires client-side two-query subtraction. Tests whether LLM recognizes this idiom.
